Hey,
I'm Harsha.
Hey, I'm Harsha. Engineer. I build backend systems, mess around with AI, and write code that hopefully works the first time. (Spoiler: it rarely does. That's fine.)

When the laptop's closed, I'm probably on a trail, staring at something wild, or three cups of coffee deep into a movie argument. Scroll down — all the serious stuff is already down there.

Work
The professional side

What I
Actually Do

Projects

Bugs Fixed
I'm a Software Engineer with 5+ years building backend systems, REST APIs, microservices, and AI-powered applications. Currently an AI Engineer at Molina Healthcare, previously an AI Engineer Intern at Hughes Network Systems where I built a RAG-powered field assistant chatbot from scratch.

I hold an MS in Computer Science from Lamar University and spend solid chunks of free time grinding LeetCode and system design — because fundamentals always show up in the work.
Core Stack
JavaPythonSpring BootAWSDockerKubernetesMongoDBMySQLKafkaOpenAI APIRAG PipelinesOpenSearchRedisREST APIsMicroservicesCI/CD
LeetCode & DSA
Consistent grinder. Heap patterns, binary trees, BFS/DFS — pattern-first over memorization. The kind of habit that actually compounds.
System Design
Working through Alex Xu Vol. 1 chapter by chapter. YouTube, Google Drive, Distributed Cache. Deep-diving into the architecture behind systems at scale.
Building Things
RAG chatbots, backend APIs, event-driven services — I like starting from zero and figuring out the whole stack. The messier the problem, the more interesting it gets.
AI & ML Engineering
Vector embeddings, semantic search, RAG pipelines. Not just calling APIs — understanding what's happening underneath.
Projects
What I've built

Projects

Real things, shipped from scratch. Each one taught me something the docs couldn't.

01
Field Technician AI Assistant
RAG-powered chatbot for field technicians. Ingests internal docs, embeds with OpenAI, stores vectors in OpenSearch, answers natural language questions with confidence thresholding. Deployed on AWS EC2.
Java 17Spring BootOpenAIOpenSearchAWSDocker
View on GitHub →
02
Student CRUD API
Production-quality Spring Boot REST API — full CRUD, JPA/Hibernate ORM, MySQL, custom queries, global exception handling, 85%+ unit test coverage. A structured deep-dive into backend fundamentals.
Spring BootMySQLJPAHibernateJUnit
View on GitHub →
03
Booking & Notification System
Event-driven booking platform using Kafka for real-time events and notifications. 500+ monthly users, 90% conflict reduction, payment integrations driving 70% adoption growth.
JavaKafkaMicroservicesSpring BootMySQL
View GitHub →
04
AI Network Monitoring System
ML-based anomaly detection pipeline on satellite telemetry data (latency, SNR, packet loss) using Scikit-learn Isolation Forest and Pandas for preprocessing. Achieved 92% anomaly detection accuracy with automated observability reporting.
PythonScikit-learnPandasIsolation ForestML
View on GitHub →

Life
Life outside the terminal

Off Screen

Okay, the resume stuff is done. Here's who I actually am.
I'm based in Austin, TX — good trails, decent coffee scene, zero whales. When I'm not debugging something at midnight, I'm outside doing something that makes my friends say "that's not normal." Fully aware. Zero regrets.

☕
Coffee
Not a personality thing — a structural dependency. Without it, I boot up in safe mode. Slow, unreliable, slightly irritable.

Gas station cup? Yes. Single-origin pour-over? Also yes. $2 cold brew from a parking lot? Absolutely yes. The bar is: does it contain caffeine. It has never failed this test.
🐍
Snakes & Reptiles
Normal people see a snake on the trail and take three steps back. I see a snake and my first thought is "wait, what species is that, let me get closer."

Rat snakes, racers, skinks, lizards — found a few around Austin and Georgetown. Every single time it's exciting. My friends don't understand it. I don't explain it anymore.
🌄
Trails & Wildlife
I am that person who stops every five minutes on a hike to flip a rock, stare at a bird, or investigate something the rest of the group has already walked past.

Pace: slow. Curiosity: dangerously high. The wilderness is basically just a bigger, buggier version of debugging — you just keep looking until you find something interesting. And you always do.
🌊
Whales (Not Yet)
I live in landlocked Austin. I have never seen a whale. This feels like a problem.

Somewhere on open water, a whale is going to surface, and I am going to be there, completely unhinged about it. I think about this more than a person in central Texas reasonably should. Bucket list. Top five. Non-negotiable.
🎬
Movies
I will watch anything. Good ones, bad ones, ones I will absolutely defend in an argument even if I'm the only one defending them.

I have a current favourite. I'm not listing it here — that's a conversation, not a bullet point. If you want to know, just ask. Fair warning: I will talk about it for a while.
🎮
Video Games
Somewhere between shipping a feature and staring at a bug at 2am, there is gaming. My brain insists it's resting. My brain is lying — it's just solving a different kind of problem.

I play across genres. I will not tell you my rank. What I will tell you is that losing to a 12-year-old online builds character.
